sentence: Subjective:
HPI: Mr. Roberts is a 49-year-old male with a history of hypertension and palpitations.
He presented to the emergency room in March with high blood pressure and palpitations, which he attributed to a panic attack triggered by caffeine consumption and lack of breakfast.
He has a family history of hypertension and has been taking medication for it.
He is currently on Benicar HCT (hydrochlorothiazide) and amlodipine.
His blood pressure has been running at 146-155 mmHg, despite taking his medications as prescribed.
Past medical history: Mr. Roberts has a history of hypertension and a family history of the same condition.
Review of systems: He denies any other symptoms.
Current medications: Amlodipine 10 milligrams daily, Benicar HCT (hydrochlorothiazide) 40-25 milligrams daily.
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient's heart and lungs sound good.
No swelling in the ankles and feet.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: Mr. Roberts presents with resistant hypertension, despite being on maximum doses of three medications.
Problem list: Hypertension; Resistant Hypertension; [NOT MENTIONED]
Plan:
I have added spironolactone to his medication regimen to address potential aldosterone-driven hypertension.
I have discussed the potential risks and side effects of the medication, including electrolyte disturbances, dehydration, and gynecomastia.
I have also instructed him to hydrate regularly and monitor his electrolyte levels.
We will schedule a follow-up appointment in two weeks to check his blood pressure and electrolytes.
If the medication does not work, we will consider referring him to the resistant hypertension clinic for further evaluation and potential treatment, including the renal denervation procedure.
Follow-up: We will see Mr. Roberts in two weeks to check his blood pressure and electrolytes.
